Name: A Wrinkle in Time
Review: This movie was, from what I've read, intended to be a movie for colored and biracial girls, which is both a good and bad thing. It's good that Disney enjoys representing equality, and the positive messages are there. But it's really confusing most of the time, and the plot is either doing too much or too little. The script is really dry as well, and the child acting was disappointing compared to most other Disney movies.

Name: Last Days of Coney Island
Review:
Is this what a New York acid trip feels like?

I can't really put a number on it, as it's a short film that doesn't really have much of a premise. But I will say that it's a really bizarre art form; it definitely conveys that message that Coney Island is a really messed up place that's been around for a lot of 20th century US History, so it accomplishes what it was supposed to.

But undoubtedly the best part of this for me was Mark Taylor's music; I'm a huge jazz fan and Mark Taylor is a great artist, so hearing him write some old New York style tunes was really awesome.

Name: Ran
Review: Spike Lee's 95 Film Recommendations Ranked I might get crucified for this, but I do think this is a bit overrated. I'm not generally a lover of long period pieces, though I did enjoy Barry Lyndon (which is better than this). Yes it's visually beautiful, and yes the acting and writing are really good. The battle sequences were also pretty great and some of the most memorable in any period piece for me. They really do kick ass. But I lost interest fairly frequently (partially thanks to my ADHD and not being able to take movies like this) and I didn't take anything that serious out of it overall, despite being entertaining to watch. The characters aren't super dynamic or memorable either, aside from the beginning scenes. I was going to comment on the score not being as memorable, but come to think of it, I actually can't remember the scores of Rashomon or Yojimbo either, and I just saw those a couple days ago. I'll be reading King Lear this year for school, so I'll definitely revisit the film after I read that. But for now, this is what I think of it.

Name: Bob le Flambeur
Review:
Pretty fun movie. Good cinematography and thoughtful writing with well-developed characters and an intriguing premise. The score was also very good, feeling like an old Disney short, and it synchronized with what the film was doing very well. You can tell the director knew what he was doing, though it didn't really do much for me by the end. I kinda just said, "That was fun... yeah!" It's not a film that I think is worth writing a lot about; though it has a lot to say, it didn't really speak to me personally. I mainly watched this because Hard Eight was very inspired by it and I'm doing a PTA binge, but it was still a pretty good movie nonetheless. Though Hard Eight is an improvement over this IMO.

Edit: The director of this has two films in the LB Top 250 I see, so I'll definitely try to learn more about Jean-Pierre Melville in the future!

Name: Ford v Ferrari
Review:
The first time I saw this in theaters almost a year ago, I was bored.

The second time I saw this today at home, I was bored.

This is overlong, dull, lacking in authenticity, annoying, and for a movie about cars, very sleep-inducing. There are so many better points for it to end, conversations that go on for too long (and aren't even very well written) and so much of the film could've been executed better but just simply wasn't. It's a frustrating movie and difficult to watch. If you enjoy it more than I do, good for you. But I don't see myself willingly re-visiting this film for quite some time, I tell you. It felt like 2019's Vice (though this is less annoying) and is, in my opinion, easily the worst of last year's best picture nominees.

Review: One of the most basic Hollywood films I've seen (so far) from 2020 but I'd be lying if I said I didn't have some good fun with the plot twists and action scenes. It's always a nice reminder that I can still enjoy some mainstream movies despite all the progress I've made this year in watching great indie and artistic stuff.

Name: The Master
Review:
Another first watch for my parents. A bit uncomfortable at times thanks to its sexual content but I'm pretty sure they liked it.

As for me... yes, it's still fantastic. Every performance is world class; especially Joaquin who gives a top 10 performance of the decade. Everything I said in my first review still stands and I think this might just be a flawless film. It's hard to say as I'm still sort of processing what I watched (again), and while I can't think of anything wrong with it per se, I just can't decide on a 9 or 10 for this film. It's honestly painful and really damn frustrating trying to decide since this film is so good but it also didn't quite blow me away like some other 10/10 films did? UGH this ranking films is hard

Name: Show People
Review:
Watched in preparation for The Artist (this was an inspiration for the film's screenplay)

The story is an interesting one, and overall this was a cool look at Hollywood in the 20s, but holy fuck did it feel slow in the first half hour. The 79 minute runtime felt much longer than that and the screenplay is rather cheesy so I hope The Artist is better written than this. But overall I'm glad I checked it out, as it tells a story as old as Hollywood itself in classic silent film fashion. Plus the cameos are great and I think the slap-stick sections are a lot of fun.

Name: Meet the Feebles
Review:
Lmao wtf?

Peter Jackson must've gone through a lot of therapy in the 90s and before making the LOTR trilogy... he went from the likes of David Lynch to Steven Spielberg in the matter of a decade.

Also this was unironically good. An insanely bizarre but intriguing portrayal of "sleazy showbiz," as the film's synopsis puts it.

Name: The Midnight Sky
Review:
I take back every mean thing I said about Interstellar.


Okay but in all seriousness, this has some pretty solid scenes and good qualities to it but nothing is consistent. One minute the film looks really nice or I care about the characters, then a really awkward transition occurs and things go back to being bleh. George Clooney's directing reminds me of Aaron Sorkin's directing but without the same skilled writer. The screenplay is so vanilla, the editing was meh, half the CGI looked fake and every green screen was obvious. There are some good looking shots but very few felt purposeful and most of them had a really distracting blur of everything in the background that just didn't feel natural at all. I also wouldn't call any of the performances great by any stretch, along with basically nonexistent character development and uncomfortable pacing that ruins any possible emotional hit with the exception of Iris and Augustine having some touching moments, but even then a lot of it is very cheesy. And while I almost never talk about a movie's music in negative light, Desplat's score felt really unoriginal and the percussion for each song in particular bothered me. Overall, The Midnight Sky feels like a film that would've been great had it come out in the late 2000s, and it was just a chore to watch (I only picked it since it's been getting awards attention for its technicals). I won't remember it in a few weeks, let alone watching it again ever.

Name: Whiplash
Review: I watched this for the first time when I was 16, and it came into my life at the exact right time. During sophomore year, I was in the top jazz band at my high school and, both with playing trumpet and everything I was doing, I was full of ambition and energy to be the best. I put too much on my plate, and when my grades fell, I found less joy in everything and I felt like a failure, I re-evaluated myself completely and burnt out. Seeing Andrew tear his life apart to get to the top just two months later burned its way into my brain forever; I saw a character that seriously reminded me of who I was at that time, and gave me some perspective on why I needed change (the entire anecdote is mostly depressing, but watching this for the first time was a good thing). The end of sophomore year was marked by COVID and quarantine, both of which did anything but help me; I was isolated from my previous life completely, left with my invasive thoughts and depression over self-esteem for a long time. I never reached the same level I was before 2020 again. Things eventually got better at the end of the year, when it was safe to go back to in-person school and jazz band, and it felt like a breath of fresh air. This fresh air lasted through senior year and into now, but I was forever a changed person. This isn't to say I miss being 16 at all; I am so proud of how far I've come and who I am today, but I'd be lying if I said I don't miss some of the excitement of being at the top of my game. I'm now in college, an aspiring filmmaker, no longer in a jazz band; things are completely different. It's a bittersweet feeling, but I contribute both strong emotions to the impact this movie has left, through all its incredible filmmaking and the personal connection it has with me. Watching this again is a big reminder of who I used to be early on in high school, and has given me some new perspective on how I have evolved as a person over the last few years. I will forever love Whiplash.
